name: Fermented Green Chutney
subtitle: Your everyday green chutney, upgraded with live cultures
blrNote: Coriander, mint, and green chillies - every BLR kitchen already has these. You make this chutney weekly anyway; now ferment it. Costs ₹15–20 for a batch from any sabziwala. At BLR's 25°C, 2 days is perfect.

IN.where: Any market - often sold alongside coriander

ingredients.name: Green chillies
substitutions: Adjust to taste - 1 for mild, 5+ for fiery

equipment.name: Glass jar (250–500ml)
equipment.notes: Small jar - this is a condiment, not a main course

tldr: Grind coriander, mint, chilli, and salt into a paste. Jar it. Wait 2 days. Your regular green chutney, now alive.

steps.title: Wash and prep
steps.instruction: Wash coriander and mint thoroughly. Remove thick stems (thin stems are fine). Roughly chop. Deseed chillies for milder heat, or leave whole for full fire.
tips: Wash well - these come from open markets and carry soil bacteria (some of which you actually want for fermentation)
Dry the greens briefly in a salad spinner or towel - excess water dilutes the chutney
Remove thick woody stems from the coriander and mint - they don't blend smoothly and leave fibrous bits in the chutney

steps.title: Grind to paste
steps.instruction: Blend coriander, mint, green chillies, salt, and optional garlic in a mixer grinder. Add minimal water - just enough to get the blades moving. You want a thick paste, not a liquid.
tips: Less water = thicker paste = better fermentation
Add lemon juice during grinding for brightness (optional at this stage)
If the blades stall, add a splash of leftover brine or whey instead of plain water - it kick-starts fermentation and keeps the paste thick

steps.title: Pack the jar
steps.instruction: Transfer the paste to a clean glass jar. Press down to remove air pockets. Leave 2cm headspace. Drizzle a thin layer of salt on top (optional, helps prevent surface mould).
tips: Pack tightly - air is the enemy of good fermentation
A thin layer of oil on top also helps seal against air
Leave at least 2cm of headspace - the paste expands slightly as fermentation produces gas, and overflow means lost chutney and a messy counter
steps.checkpoint: Jar packed with dense green paste

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely. Leave at room temperature for 2–3 days. The chutney will darken slightly and develop a tangy, complex flavour distinct from fresh chutney. Small bubbles may appear.
tips: Day 2 in BLR is usually the sweet spot - tangy but still fresh-tasting
The colour will shift from bright green to a deeper, olive green - normal
If it turns brown-black or smells off, discard - this means oxidation or contamination
steps.checkpoint: Slightly darker green, distinctly tangy, complex aroma

steps.title: Refrigerate and use
steps.instruction: Seal and refrigerate. Use as you would regular green chutney - with dosa, idli, pakora, sandwiches, chaat, or stirred into rice.
tips: Keeps 2–3 weeks refrigerated (longer than fresh chutney)
The tang deepens over days - taste evolves
steps.checkpoint: Living green chutney with probiotic tang

images.caption: Fermented Green Chutney
images.alt: Fermented Green Chutney - Your everyday green chutney, upgraded with live cultures

culturalContext.story: Green chutney - coriander, mint, chilli, ground together - is the universal condiment of the Indian subcontinent. It appears at every chaat stall, alongside every dosa, inside every sandwich in Mumbai. Usually consumed fresh, it has a shelf life of just 2–3 days in the fridge. Fermenting it deliberately extends its life AND adds probiotic benefit - turning something ephemeral into something alive.
culturalContext.historicalNote: The practice of grinding herbs with salt and fermenting them is ancient across South Asia. Before blenders, stone mortars (sil-batta) were used. The fermentation step was often unintentional - chutneys made in bulk would naturally ferment in warm weather. Many grandmothers' "special flavour" was, unknowingly, lacto-fermentation.
culturalContext.significance: This recipe is about reframing something you already make. Every Bangalorean household has green chutney in their routine. Adding a 2-day fermentation step requires no new skills, no new ingredients, no new equipment - just patience. It's the lowest-barrier entry point to fermentation for anyone in India.
culturalContext.funFact: In a 2018 food science study, lacto-fermented herb pastes showed 10x higher bioavailability of iron and zinc compared to fresh versions. The fermentation breaks down phytic acid - the same compound that makes spinach iron "less available" than it appears on paper.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Colour change is normal
thingsToAccountFor.description: Fermented green chutney will darken from bright green to olive-khaki. This is chlorophyll breaking down in the acidic environment. It's cosmetic, not harmful. Taste, don't judge by colour alone.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Minimal water is key
thingsToAccountFor.description: Too much water in the paste invites contamination and weakens the ferment. The paste should be thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon.

thingsToAccountFor.title: BLR summer speed
thingsToAccountFor.description: In April–May, when BLR hits 30°C+, this can over-ferment in 36 hours. Start checking early and refrigerate when tangy.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Spread fermented chutney thinly on parchment. Dehydrate at 40°C for 8–10 hours. Crumble or powder.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Fermented green chutney powder - tangy, herby, probiotic-adjacent seasoning for rice, raita, popcorn.
dehydratorIntegration.shelfLife: 3–4 months airtight in a cool, dry place
tips: Spread as thin as possible for even drying
Grind to powder and mix with salt for an incredible chutney salt

variations.name: Curry leaf fermented chutney
variations.description: Add a handful of fresh curry leaves (karibevu) to the blend. Deepens the South Indian character.

variations.name: Coconut green chutney (fermented)
variations.description: Add 2 tbsp fresh grated coconut to the blend. Richer, more Karnataka-style. Ferments similarly.

variations.name: Spicy fermented chutney
variations.description: Double the green chillies. Add raw mango (when in season) for extra sour punch. Not for the faint-hearted.
